serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Drunken Chicken is approximately 150 g, representing one portion of cold marinated chicken pieces as served at a banquet or family meal.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, broilers or fryers, thigh, meat and skin, cooked, roasted — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 172079)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Drunken Chicken (Zui Ji) exists in USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked chicken thigh (meat and skin, FDC ID 172079) as the closest approved-source equivalent, with sodium adjusted upward to reflect the Shaoxing wine and salt marinade (~280 mg/100 g). Input AI-generated values were cross-checked against this USDA anchor and found broadly consistent; USDA-anchored values are used as the authoritative basis. Saturated fat estimated at 2.4 g/100 g from USDA cooked chicken thigh fatty acid profile.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — negligible carbohydrate content; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: Drunken Chicken contains only ~1.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic index is not applicable for this high-protein, moderate-fat dish with negligible carbohydrates. No GI testing is performed on foods with negligible carbohydrate content per University of Sydney GI Database methodology. GL per 150 g serving is effectively 0.0. Reference to University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) cited as the authoritative GI methodology source confirming that GI testing is not applicable for foods with negligible carbohydrate content.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from salt and Shaoxing wine marinade (~280 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should monitor portion size, as a 150 g serving contributes approximately 420 mg sodium; request reduced-salt preparation or limit frequency if sodium intake is already near the daily 2000 mg target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~1.5 g/100 g) with high protein supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Drunken Chicken is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible carbohydrate load and high protein content, which supports postprandial blood glucose control; a standard 150 g serving contributes fewer than 3 g of carbohydrate.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (~8.5 g/100 g) with estimated saturated fat ~2.4 g/100 g; lean poaching method limits added fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The poaching preparation method avoids added cooking fats, and the saturated fat content is moderate; individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume this dish in standard portions as part of a balanced diet, while remaining mindful of cumulative sodium intake from the marinade.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lean poached chicken provides anti-inflammatory protein without high pro-inflammatory saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Poached chicken in this dish is a lean protein source with no high pro-inflammatory fat profile; suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as part of a balanced diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Drunken Chicken (醉鸡, Zuìjī) is a classic cold appetizer originating from Zhejiang Province in the Jiangnan region of eastern China. The dish is prepared by first poaching whole chicken or chicken thighs with ginger and scallions until just tender, then steeping the cooked meat in a fragrant marinade of high-quality Shaoxing rice wine, salt, sugar, and optional aromatics such as goji berries or dried dates. The chicken is chilled for several hours or overnight, allowing it to absorb the wine's subtle sweetness and floral fragrance. The resulting dish features silky, succulent meat with a lightly gelatinous skin and a delicate wine-infused flavour. Nutritionally, Drunken Chicken is high in protein (~18.5 g/100 g), moderate in fat (~8.5 g/100 g), and very low in carbohydrates (~1.5 g/100 g), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or following low-carbohydrate diets. Sodium content (~280 mg/100 g) warrants attention for those managing hypertension. The dish is a popular starter at festive banquets and family gatherings, particularly during summer months. It is not suitable for those on strictly alcohol-free diets due to the Shaoxing wine marinade.
common_user_queries: Is drunken chicken suitable for diabetics?
What wine is used in drunken chicken?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Drunken Chicken (Zui Ji) exists in any approved primary source (USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked chicken thigh (meat and skin, FDC ID 172079) as the closest approved-source equivalent and cross-checked against the AI-generated input values, which were broadly consistent. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cooked chicken thigh, meat and skin, FDC ID 172079) as no exact approved-source entry exists for Drunken Chicken. Input AI-generated values were consistent with this anchor and have been retained with USDA as the authoritative basis. 2. Saturated fat estimated at 2.4 g/100 g based on USDA cooked chicken thigh fatty acid profile. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, cholesterol, and moisture data for this specific wine-marinated cold preparation are not individually reported in USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 172079) or other approved sources as of 2026; these values cannot be reliably derived without a specific laboratory analysis of the marinated preparation. 4. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed as naturally zero for a pure poached chicken and wine marinade dish with no plant fibre sources. 5. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (~1.5 g/100 g); gi_category set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index set to null per q4 rules for negligible-carbohydrate foods. GL per serving is 0.0. 6. The dish contains Shaoxing rice wine and is not suitable for strictly alcohol-free diets; halal status is false due to alcohol content. 7. Ontology mapped to FoodOn broader term 'chicken food product' (FOODON_03302051) and FoodEx2 A00GJ (chicken meat, cooked) as no specific codes exist for this preparation; gap notes provided for both. 8. The input source (Zhejiang University Food Science Department) is not on the approved source list and has not been used as primary evidence. 9. GI source references University of Sydney GI Database methodology and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283) confirming GI testing is not applicable for foods with negligible carbohydrate content.
